1: YHWH's call "return to me" (not merely "return") frames repentance as a personal return to the God of the covenant. Christ is both the one who issues this call as YHWH's incarnate Word (John 1:14) and the one who makes the return possible: he comes down to meet those who cannot find their way back. His parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:20) is the enacted form of this verse — the father running toward the returning son is YHWH of Jeremiah 4:1.
2: "In truth, justice, and righteousness" — the three covenant virtues that Israel's oaths lack. Christ embodies all three: he is the truth (John 14:6), he brings justice to the nations (Matthew 12:18-21, citing Isa 42), and he is made righteousness for us (1 Corinthians 1:30). The oath "as the LORD lives" sworn in these three qualities is the oath the nations will one day swear sincerely (Isaiah 45:23; Romans 14:11) — a promise fulfilled as Christ draws the nations to himself.
3: "Break up your untilled ground and do not sow among thorns." Jesus's parable of the sower (Matthew 13:3-9) takes up this exact agricultural metaphor: the rocky, thorny ground that chokes the seed is the untilled, uncircumcised heart. The preparation of the soil corresponds to the work of the Spirit that precedes repentance. Christ himself is the sower who comes to sow the word; the call of Jeremiah 4:3 is realized when the word of the kingdom takes root.
4: Circumcise your hearts — the inner circumcision that Deuteronomy 10:16 and 30:6 anticipate. Paul reads this trajectory in Romans 2:28-29: "A person is not a Jew who is one outwardly... circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code." Colossians 2:11-12 identifies this heart-circumcision with baptism into Christ's death and resurrection — the circumcision "not done by human hands." Christ performs the true circumcision Jeremiah demands: the cutting away of the old nature through his cross.
5: The trumpet-call to gather in the fortified cities sounds the alarm of approaching judgment. Matthew 24:31 and 1 Corinthians 15:52 use the same trumpet-and-gathering imagery for Christ's return — not to flee into cities but to gather the elect. The same alarm that signals judgment here becomes the signal of final redemption when Christ sounds his trumpet. The gathered remnant finds their city not in Zion's fortifications but in Christ himself (Hebrews 12:22-24).
6: "Raise the signal flag toward Zion; flee to safety." The signal flag (nēs) lifted toward Zion becomes in Isaiah's vision the banner around which the nations gather (Isaiah 11:10, 12) — and Paul identifies this banner with Christ (Romans 15:12). The escape flag that here signals imminent doom becomes in Christ the rallying point for final rescue. The one who was lifted up (John 12:32 — "I, when I am lifted up, will draw all people to myself") is the ultimate signal toward which all nations flee for safety.
7: "The lion has come out of his thicket, and the destroyer of nations has set out." The coming destroyer is YHWH's instrument of judgment (Babylon). Christ enters this picture as the one who absorbs the destroyer's blow rather than routing it away: he stands in the path of the lion and is struck (cf. 1 Peter 5:8 — the adversary prowls like a roaring lion; Christ has already defeated this roar, 1 Corinthians 15:55). The city laid waste in Jeremiah's day anticipates the city Christ redeems at final cost.
8: "Put on sackcloth and wail and howl; for the fierce anger of the LORD has not turned back." The divine wrath that has not turned back in Jeremiah's generation was absorbed — not turned back — in the death of Christ. The anger settled fully on him (Romans 3:25; 1 John 2:2): at Calvary the anger is finally and finally satisfied rather than merely postponed. The mourning of Jeremiah 4:8 finds its resolution in the mourning of those who look on the one they have pierced (Zechariah 12:10) — mourning that turns to eschatological comfort.
9: "The king's courage will fail, along with the courage of the officials and the priests." Every human institution of leadership fails before the final judgment. Christ is the King whose courage did not fail — who "set his face like flint" (Isaiah 50:7) toward Jerusalem and the cross. He is also the great High Priest who entered the most holy place not trembling but with full access (Hebrews 4:14-16; Jeremiah 10:19-22). Where Judah's kings and priests lose heart, Christ holds the priestly-kingly office with unwavering resolve.
10: "You have truly deceived this people." Jeremiah's protest that the false peace-promises constituted a divine deception is his boldest complaint. Christ does not deceive: he is the truth (John 14:6), and his warnings are always fully honest (Matthew 24:4 — "See that no one deceives you"). He bore the consequences of the deceptions Israel experienced — taking the judgment that came because they believed lies — while himself speaking only truth. His integrity under false accusation (Mark 14:55-59) is the antithesis of the false promises Jeremiah protests.
11: "A scorching wind from the bare heights in the desert — not to winnow and not to cleanse." A wind of pure judgment, not the productive wind of harvest. The Spirit comes in wind (Acts 2:2 — "a sound like the rushing of a mighty wind") and his wind is for cleansing and new creation, not destruction. Christ sends the purifying Spirit where the scorching judgment-wind of Jeremiah 4:11 would have destroyed; the same divine breath that consumes opposition becomes life-giving wind for those who are in Christ.
12: YHWH pronounces judgment through the scorching wind. The language of judicial pronouncement — "now I will pronounce judgment against them" — is the language of the last assize. Christ is the judge before whom all such judgments reach their final form (John 5:22 — the Father has given all judgment to the Son). The partial judgments of history (Babylon against Jerusalem) are anticipatory verdicts that point to the comprehensive final judgment Christ pronounces.
13: "Look — he advances like storm clouds; his chariots are like a whirlwind." The terrifying advance of Babylon; yet Revelation 19:11 depicts Christ himself riding out on a white horse with the armies of heaven. The imagery of the advancing warrior-judge that Babylon embodies in judgment becomes, at the end, the very image of Christ as the final judge whose coming cannot be stopped. The storm-cloud advance terrifies those outside covenant; for those in Christ it is his appearing in glory (Titus 2:13).
14: "O Jerusalem, wash the evil from your heart." The washing Christ provides is not moral effort but the blood that cleanses: 1 John 1:7 — "the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin." Titus 3:5 speaks of the "washing of rebirth and renewing by the Holy Spirit." The call of Jeremiah 4:14 (wash!) is answered by Christ's atoning work: he provides what the imperative demands. The one who said "unless I wash you, you have no part with me" (John 13:8) supplies the washing the heart cannot perform on itself.
15: The herald voices from Dan and Mount Ephraim — the northern border — announce that judgment is approaching from the direction of Babylon. These are the watchmen of impending doom. Christ is the final herald-prophet announcing not coming judgment but accomplished salvation and impending consummation. His proclamation in Luke 4:18 ("the Spirit of the Lord is on me, to proclaim good news") is the last and greatest herald-voice that answers the herald-voices of Jeremiah's day.
16: The besiegers announced to the nations — Jerusalem is surrounded. The nations who "hear" this as spectators of judgment eventually become, in Christ, participants in Jerusalem's salvation (Ephesians 2:12-13 — those who were once "foreigners to the covenants of the promise" are now brought near). The nations who watched Babylon besiege Jerusalem anticipate the nations who through Christ are grafted into the covenant city (Revelation 21:24-26).
17: "Like guards surrounding a field, so they surround her on every side, because she has rebelled against me." The siege as covenant judgment — rebellion brings encirclement. Christ bore the ultimate encirclement: surrounded by his enemies at Gethsemane and Golgotha, he experienced the siege that covenant rebellion deserved. His death in the surrounded city (Jerusalem) absorbs the covenant curse of encirclement (Deuteronomy 28:52) on behalf of those he rescues.
18: "Your conduct and your actions have brought this on you." The covenant justice: the punishment fits the crime. Paul's principle in Galatians 6:7 ("whatever a person sows, they will also reap") expresses the same theology. Christ enters this framework as the one who reaps what he did not sow — bearing the consequences of others' conduct (Isaiah 53:4-5: "he took up our pain and bore our suffering... the punishment that brought us peace was on him"). He breaks the equation of Jeremiah 4:18 by absorbing results he did not cause.
19: "My anguish, my anguish! I writhe in pain!" Jeremiah's visceral identification with the suffering of the people — the prophet's own body resonating with the nation's agony. This is one of the deepest anticipations of Christ's incarnational empathy. Luke 19:41 — Jesus "wept over" Jerusalem; John 11:35 — "Jesus wept." The prophet who cannot keep silent in his anguish prefigures the Christ who weeps with those who weep (Romans 12:15) and whose suffering is not distant sympathy but physical identification. Hebrews 5:7 describes Jesus offering up "loud cries and tears."
20: "Crash after crash — the whole land is laid waste; in a flash my tents are destroyed." The rapid collapse of everything — the speed of total loss. The eschatological dimension: 2 Peter 3:10 speaks of the heavens disappearing with a roar and the elements being destroyed. Christ addresses this instability with the promise that his word will never pass away (Matthew 24:35) and that the final collapse of all earthly structures is not the end but the threshold of the new creation (Revelation 21:1).
21: "How long must I see the battle standard and hear the blare of the trumpet?" The prophet's lament at the unending warfare. Revelation uses both battle-standard and trumpet extensively for the end-time sequence (Rev 8-11, trumpet judgments; Rev 19, the war). Christ's "how long" language appears in Mark 9:19 ("how long shall I put up with you?") — the same divine frustration at human faithlessness that runs through Jeremiah. His coming answers the "how long" of all Jeremianic lament.
22: "My people are foolish — they do not know me; they are clever at doing evil." This is the inversion that Paul diagnoses in 1 Corinthians 1:25: "the foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom." The people who are stupid about good and clever about evil are saved by one who appears foolish by their standards — the crucified Christ who confounds the clever (1 Corinthians 1:27-29). Christ is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:30) who replaces the false cleverness of those who do not know YHWH.
23: "I looked at the earth and it was tōhû wābōhû — formless and void." The deliberate reversal of Genesis 1:2 — the uncreation of the ordered world through covenant violation. The same vocabulary makes this the most theologically charged vision in Jeremiah: sin undoes creation. Christ's redemption is explicitly new-creation language: "if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come; the old has gone, the new is here" (2 Corinthians 5:17). The tōhû wābōhû of judgment is answered by the new creation of resurrection.
24: The quaking mountains and swaying hills — creation destabilized by the weight of divine judgment. Hebrews 12:26-28 quotes the Sinai earthquake ("I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens") and applies it to the final shaking that removes what can be shaken, leaving only the unshakeable kingdom. Christ is the one who stands unshaken while all else falls, and those in him share his unshakeable ground. The mountains that quake in Jeremiah 4:24 yield ultimately to the mountain that cannot be moved — Zion in its eschatological form (Hebrews 12:22).
25: "I looked — there was no human being, and all the birds of the sky had flown away." Total depopulation — the reversal of Genesis 1:20-28 (birds created, humanity mandated to fill the earth). The cosmic scope of judgment mirrors the cosmic scope of the image of God in humanity: when humanity falls, creation falls with it (Romans 8:20-22 — creation subjected to futility because of human sin). Christ as the last Adam (1 Corinthians 15:45) restores the human vocation and with it creation's glory.
26: "The fertile land was a desert; all its cities lay in ruins before the LORD." The Carmel become wilderness — the covenantal reversal of blessing to curse (Deuteronomy 28:23-24). Christ restores this reversal: Isaiah 35 (wilderness blooming) is Jesus's own answer to John the Baptist's question (Matthew 11:5 — "the blind see, the lame walk, the dead are raised"). His healings and the feeding of crowds in the wilderness are the signs that the wilderness-to-Carmel reversal has begun in him.
27: "Yet I will not make a complete end." The kālâ-clause — YHWH will not make a total annihilation. This remnant-guarantee within the judgment oracle is the theological hinge of the whole chapter. Paul quotes this verse directly in Romans 9:28 ("the Lord will carry out his sentence on earth with speed and finality") in the context of arguing that not all Israel is lost — Christ preserves a remnant. The incomplete end that Jeremiah promises is the opening for redemption that Christ enters.
28: "For this the earth will mourn and the heavens above grow dark; for I have spoken, I have decided, and I will not turn back." The cosmic mourning of creation at covenant rupture. At the crucifixion, darkness covered the land from noon until three (Matthew 27:45) and the earth shook (Matthew 27:51) — creation itself responding to the death of the Son with the cosmic signs of covenant judgment being absorbed. The irreversible divine decree of Jeremiah 4:28 is met by the equally irreversible divine decision of the cross.
29: Every city abandoned; hiding in thickets and rocks. Revelation 6:15-17 describes the same flight — "the kings of the earth, the princes, the generals... hid in caves and among the rocks of the mountains" at the Lamb's wrath. The covering requested (Revelation 6:16 — "fall on us and hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne and from the wrath of the Lamb") is the very thing Christ provides for those who come to him — not hiding from the wrath but being sheltered within the one who absorbed it.
30: "Though you clothe yourself in crimson, though you adorn yourself with golden jewelry... you adorn yourself in vain" — the futility of Jerusalem's self-beautification before judgment. The Bride of Revelation 19:7-8 is clothed not in her own crimson but in "fine linen, bright and clean" — "the righteous acts of God's holy people." The clothing that adorns in vain in Jeremiah is replaced by the righteousness of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:21) that constitutes the true beauty of the eschatological bride.
31: "The cry of Daughter Zion gasping, stretching out her hands: Woe to me, for I am fainting before killers!" The birthing-cry and death-cry together — the city in extremis. The lament of Zion at her killers is the lament Christ hears and answers: Luke 23:28 — Jesus says to the daughters of Jerusalem, "weep for yourselves and for your children." His death in Jerusalem takes the city's death-cry into himself; his resurrection is the city's cry answered with new life. Revelation 12:2 (the woman crying out in labor) takes up this cosmic birth-and-death imagery and resolves it in Christ's victory.
